title: AI is the greatest money-wasting scheme humanity has ever invented
gist: A reposted Cory Doctorow polemic arguing AI is a real but plateaued technology wrapped in the largest and most dangerous investment bubble in history: ~35% of the S&P 500 sits in seven firms (six unprofitable on AI), ~$1.4tn capex chases <$60bn turnover, and unlike the web AI has inverted unit economics where every prompt loses money.

insights.claim: Doctorow claims 35% of the S&P 500 is tied up in seven firms, six of which have yet to make a profit on AI, with only Nvidia profitable because the other six funnel their investors' money to it.
insights.speaker: Xebec
insights.so_what: Frames index concentration as a single correlated AI bet; a ~35% cohort collapse is the systemic tail-risk for passive S&P 500 holders.
insights.quote: 35 per cent of the S&P 500 is tied up in seven firms, six of which have yet to make a penny’s profit on AI. (Nvidia, the seventh one, is profitable, because it’s the company the other six have given all their investors’ money to.)

insights.claim: AI capex stands at $1.4tn and counting against an industry with annual global turnover of less than $60bn, which Doctorow argues could make it the most toxic investment bubble in history.
insights.speaker: Xebec
insights.so_what: A ~23x capex-to-revenue gap is the core bear quantification; if revenue doesn't catch up, write-downs across the AI capex chain follow.
insights.quote: At $1.4tn in capital expenditure and counting, this industry – with an annual global turnover of less than $60bn – could become the most toxic investment bubble in history.
insights.locator: AI then is the greatest money-wasting venture...

insights.claim: Meta's AI bill for the past three years sits around $150bn — versus the ~$60bn it spent on the metaverse — and management claims it will double that total this year.
insights.speaker: Xebec
insights.so_what: Concrete gauge of accelerating, unproven AI spend at a single hyperscaler; doubling the bill raises the stakes on Meta's AI ROI proof.
insights.quote: Meta’s AI bill for the past three years sits around $150bn, and they claim they’ll double that total this year.

themes.theme: Inverted unit economics vs. the web

insights.claim: Doctorow argues AI's unit economics are the inverse of the web's: every new web user made it more profitable, whereas every new AI user and every prompt makes the AI sector lose more money, and each AI generation loses more than the last.
insights.speaker: Xebec
insights.so_what: Central thesis against the dotcom analogy: scale worsens AI losses, so growth alone won't bail out valuations the way it did for the web.
insights.quote: Every new AI user makes the AI sector lose more money... Every new generation of AI loses more money than the last.
insights.locator: Unlike AI, the web had brilliant unit economics.

insights.claim: Doctorow contends no one is willing to pay the true cost of AI tokens, and as the giants prepare to float and clean up balance sheets by raising prices, their largest enterprise customers are imposing strict limits on AI usage.
insights.speaker: Xebec
insights.so_what: Enterprise usage caps under rising token prices is a demand-elasticity warning; watch token-price hikes and enterprise consumption as bubble-stress signals ahead of AI IPOs.
insights.quote: As these giants get ready for their flotation and attempt to clean up their balance sheets by raising prices, their largest enterprise customers are imposing strict limits on AI usage.

insights.claim: Doctorow concedes AI is real machine-learning progress but argues results have plateaued and that, absent the bubble, current AI products would simply be called 'plug-ins' rather than a $1.4tn industry.
insights.speaker: Xebec
insights.so_what: If scaling returns have plateaued, the valuation case built on continued capability gains is undermined regardless of the tech's genuine utility.
insights.quote: But these results have since plateaued, and anything that can’t go on forever eventually stops.
insights.locator: I’m not saying AI is not real.

insights.claim: Doctorow argues AI attracted far more capital than prior bubbles (metaverse, crypto, NFTs) because it sells the ideology of a world without people — letting bosses and officials bypass workers and civil servants.
insights.speaker: Xebec
insights.so_what: Explains the demand-side narrative durability; the labor-replacement promise sustains capital inflows independent of unit economics, prolonging the bubble.
insights.quote: AI dangles the possibility of a world without people.
insights.locator: To understand why the AI bubble has attracted so much more capital...
